Resources are assigned a duration calculation mode based on the resource type assigned to the 
resource. This mode identifies how the primary resource calculates durations for operation 
segments. When this mode is set to Rate, the production rate of the resource must be specified. 
 1. Click the value in the Is Rate=0 and Duration Calculation Mode=Rate? column to open 
the Resources worksheet.
 2. Specify a value  in the Rate (per day) column.
Secondary resource group type and Resource type class mismatch
Resources must be assigned to the appropriate resource group. A mismatch of a resource type 
and resource group type are flagged. 

===== PAGE 212 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Applicationextensions 202
 1. Click the value in the Is Resource Class = Secondary and ResourceGroup Type = 
Primary? column to open the Resource Group to Resource worksheet.
 2. Delete the mismatched record and create a new record for that secondary resource and 
assign it to an appropriate secondary resource group.
Route
Route operation predecessors with min wait duration > max wait 
duration
Route operation predecessors define the precedence constraints between route operation steps. 
How operations are scheduled in relation to one another is determined by the assigned 
operation predecessor type and the defined wait durations and quantities. Predecessors can't 
have a minimum wait time that is greater than the maximum wait time. 
 1. Click the value in the Predecessor Route Operation column to open the Route 
Operation Predecessor worksheet.
 2. Edit the values in the Maximum Wait Duration (days) and/or Minimum Wait Duration 
(days) columns. 
Route operation segments with duration <= 0 and rate <= 0
Route operation segments must have duration and rate values that are greater than 0.
 1. Click the value in the Is duration <=0 and rate <=0? column to open the Route 
Operation Segment worksheet.
 2. Modify the Duration (days) and/or Rate (per day) values accordingly.
Route Operations mapped to >1 operation specifications
A single operation specification can be applied to multiple route operations, however each route 
operation can only be mapped to one operation specification.
 1. Click the value in the Is mapped to multiple Operation Specifications column to open 
the Route Operation To Operation Specification worksheet.
 2. Remove the additional records for the flagged route operation.
Route Operations with segment duration = 0 and Duration Calculation 
Mode = Duration
The duration calculation mode identifies how a primary resource calculates durations for 
operation segments. When this mode is set to Duration, route operation segments must have a 
duration value greater than 0.

===== PAGE 213 =====
203 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER3:EnterpriseSchedulingresources
 1. Click the value in the Is duration=0 and duration calculation mode=duration? column 
to open the Route Operation worksheet.
 2. Specify a value greater than 0 in the Duration (days) column.
Route operations without mapped segment
Route operations are divided into segments that make up the individual steps for an operation. 
Each route operation must have one or more segments defined. 
 1. Click the value in the Has segments? column to open the Route Operation Segment 
worksheet.
 2. Insert a new record  for that route operation.
Routes without mapped route operation
Routes are divided into one or more route operations that represent a step in the production 
process. Each route must have one or more route operations defined. 
 1. Click the value in the  Has operations? column to open the Route Operation worksheet.
 2. Edit or insert a new record to define a route operation for the route.
Scheduled Receipt
No SR operations exist for site
Scheduled Receipt operations must have an order site specified. 
 1. Click the value in the Is SR Operation Available? column to open the Scheduled 
Receipts worksheet.
 2. Click the value in the Operations column for the site or sites with no scheduled receipt 
operations.
 3. In the Scheduled Receipt Operations worksheet, edit or add a new record that assigns a 
scheduled receipt operation to the site.
SR operation predecessors with min wait duration > max wait 
duration
Scheduled receipt operation predecessors define the precedence constraints between scheduled 
receipt operation steps. How operations are scheduled in relation to one another is determined 
by the assigned operation predecessor type and the defined wait durations and quantities. 
Predecessors can't have a minimum wait time that is greater than the maximum wait time. 
 1. Click the value in the Is min wait duration > max wait duration column to open the 
Scheduled Receipt Operation Predecessor worksheet.

===== PAGE 214 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Applicationextensions 204
 2. Edit the values in the Maximum Wait Duration and/or Minimum Wait - Duration 
columns. 
SR operation predecessors with min wait quantity > scheduled receipt 
quantity
The minimum wait quantity for a scheduled receipt operation can't be more than the quantity for 
the scheduled receipt. 
 1. Click he value in the Is min wait Qty > SR Qty column to open the Scheduled Receipt 
Operation Predecessor worksheet.
 2. Edit the values in the Maximum Wait - Quantity column. 
SR operation predecessors with the same Predecessor SR operation 
and SR operation
Scheduled receipt operation predecessors define the precedence constraints between scheduled 
receipt operation steps. How operations are scheduled in relation to one another is determined 
by the assigned predecessor type and the defined wait durations and quantities. A predecessor 
operation must have a different operation defined as it successor operation.
 1. Click the value in the Is Predecessor SR Operation same as SR Operation? column to 
open the Scheduled Receipt Operation Predecessor worksheet.
 2. Edit the Predecessor Scheduled Receipt and Scheduled Receipt column values 
accordingly.
SR Operations mapped to >1 operation specifications
A single operation specification can be applied to multiple scheduled receipt operations, 
however each scheduled receipt operation can only be mapped to one operation specification.
 1. Click the value in the Is mapped to multiple Operation Specifications column to open 
the Scheduled Receipt Operation To Operation Specification worksheet in the 
Enterprise Scheduling Demand workbook.
 2. Remove the additional records for the flagged scheduled receipt operation.
SR operations that are Locked, but without an input end date time
The input end date time defines when the operation is scheduled to end.
 1. Click the value in the Locked operation without - input end date column ? to open the 
Scheduled Receipt Operations worksheet.
 2. Specify a value in the Input End Date Time column.

===== PAGE 215 =====
205 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER3:EnterpriseSchedulingresources
SR operations that are Locked, but without an input primary resource
The input primary resource identifies the primary input resources associated with the scheduled 
receipt operation.
 1. Click the value in the Locked operation without - input primary resource? to open the 
Scheduled Receipt Operations worksheet.
 2. Specify values for the Input Primary Resource columns.
SR Operations with 0 quantity
Scheduled receipt operations are the production steps take to fulfill a scheduled receipt. Each 
scheduled receipt operation must have a quantity value greater than 0.
 1. Click the value in the Quantity column to open the Scheduled Receipt Operations 
worksheet.
 2. Specify a quantity value greater than 0.
SR operations with either actual start datetime or end datetime but 
without primary resource
Scheduled Receipt operations with defined date and time values must be assigned to a primary 
resource that they start and/or end production on. 
 1. Click the value in the Without primary resource? column to open the Scheduled 
Receipt Operations worksheet.
 2. Specify values for the Primary Resource columns.
SR operations without segment
Scheduled Receipt operations are made up of segments such as loading or working that make 
up the individual steps for the operation. Each scheduled receipt operation must have one or 
more segments defined. 
 1. Click the value in the Has segments? column to open the Scheduled Receipt Operations 
worksheet.
 2. Edit or insert a new record in the Route Operation Segment worksheet for that 
scheduled receipt operation.

===== PAGE 216 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Applicationextensions 206
Search Algorithm
Area groups having resources tagged Schedule by Search Algorithm 
without objective function linked
Area groups with resources groups that have the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox 
selected must have an Objective Function specified. 
 1. Click the value in the Mapped by Search Algorithm Resource without Objective 
Function column to open the Resource to Resource Group worksheet.
 2. Clear the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox for any resource groups in that area 
group.
Area groups having resources tagged Schedule by Search Algorithm 
without search algorithm rule linked
Area groups with resources groups that have the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox 
selected must have a Search Algorithm Rule Set specified. 
 1. Click the value in the Mapped by Search Algorithm Resource without algorithm rule 
column to open the Resource to Resource Group worksheet.
 2. Clear the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox for any resource groups in that area 
group.
Area groups with more than one primary resource group tagged 
Schedule by Search Algorithm
Area groups can only have one primary resource group with the Schedule by Search Algorithm 
checkbox selected. 
 1. Click the value in the Mapped by Search Algorithm - Resource Group column to open 
the Resource to Resource Group worksheet.
 2. Clear the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox for the additional primary resource 
groups.
Invalid Search Algorithm rule
The Search Algorithm rule is invalid because of one of the following conditions:
 l StartIteration or EndIteration < 0
 l StartIteration > EndIteration
 l StartIteration  = 0 AND EndIteration != 0
 l StartIteration  != 0 AND EndIteration = 0
 l Probability < 0 or > 1

===== PAGE 217 =====
207 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER3:EnterpriseSchedulingresources
 1. Click the value in the Invalid Search Algorithm Rule? column to open the Search 
Algorithm Rule Component worksheet.
 2. Review the parameters to identify and fix which condition above triggered the integrity 
issue.
Maximum iterations <= 1,000 and Search Algorithm rule component 
is not InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement
The Search Algorithm rule component is set to InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement but 
the maximum iterations is set to <= 1,000. This is an issue because these component types 
require larger numbers of iterations to succeed at improving the schedule.
 1. Click the value in the Maximum iterations <= 1,000 with non-improvement 
components column to open the Search Algorithm Rule Component worksheet.
 2. Do one of the following:
 l In the Search Algorithm - Component Type column, change the value to something 
other than InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement.
 l In the Area Groups worksheet, set the value for Search Algorithm - Maximum 
Iterations to greater than 1,000.
Maximum iterations > 1,000 and the scoring mode is 
SchedulingEngine
When the scoring mode for a Search Algorithm Rule is set to SchedulingEngine, the Scheduling 
algorithm is run each time a candidate schedule is generated. When the maximum iterations are 
set to more than 1,000 iterations this is logged as an issue because of performance.
 1. Click the value in the Maximum iterations > 1,000 with SchedulingEngine mode 
column to open the Search Algorithm Rule  worksheet.
 2. Do one of the following:
 l In the Search Algorithm - Scoring Mode column, change the value to Approximate.
 l In the Area Groups worksheet, set the value for Search Algorithm - Maximum 
Iterations to less than 1,000.
Maximum iterations > 100,000,000, Search Algorithm rule component 
is not InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement, and the scoring 
mode is not SchedulingEngine
When the scoring mode for a Search Algorithm rule is not set to SchedulingEngine, the 
component type isn't InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement, and the maximum iterations 
is set to > 100 thousand, an issue is logged. 

===== PAGE 218 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Applicationextensions 208
 1. Click the value in the Maximum iterations > 100,000,000 with non-improvement 
components column to open the Search Algorithm Rule  worksheet.
 2. Do one of the following:
 l In the Search Algorithm - Scoring Mode column, change the value to Approximate.
 l In the Search Algorithm Rule Component worksheet, for the Search Algorithm -
 Component Type column, change the value to something other than 
InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement.
 l In the Area Groups worksheet, set the value for Search Algorithm - Maximum 
Iterations to less than 100,000,000.
No maximum runtime set for Search Algorithm
The maximum runtime defines the maximum number of seconds that the algorithm can run. This 
value is checked every iteration so the limit can be exceeded by the duration of a single iteration 
of the algorithm. A value set at <=0 implies that there is no time limit.
 1. Click the value in the No maximum runtime set for Search algorithm column to open 
the Area Groups worksheet
 2. Specify a value in the Search Algorithm - Maximum Runtime column.
Objective function without component
Objective functions must have at least one component defined. 
 1. Click the value in the Has component column? column to open the Objective Function 
Component worksheet.
 2. Insert a new record to define a component for that objective function.
Perturbation intensity factor <= 0
The perturbation intensity factor determines the number of perturbations that happen for each 
iteration of the schedule. 
 1. Click the value in the Perturbation intensity factor <= 0? column to open the Search 
Algorithm Rule worksheet.
 2. Specify a value greater than 0 in the Perturbation Intensity Factor column. The 
recommended value is 1. Note that higher values introduce more randomness in the 
search.
Resource groups with Type as Secondary and tagged Schedule by 
Search Algorithm
Resource groups with only secondary resources can't be scheduled using the Search Algorithm 
option.  in the Resource Groups worksheet for this group.

===== PAGE 219 =====
209 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER3:EnterpriseSchedulingresources
 1. Click the value in the Is secondary resource tagged Schedule by Search Algorithm? 
column to open the Resource Groups worksheet.
 2. Clear the Schedule by Search Algorithm checkbox for all applicable resource groups.
Search Algorithm rule component is InsertionImprovement or 
SwapImprovement and (eligible schedule receipt operations * total 
resources in resource group) > 1,000
The Search Algorithm rule component is set to InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement and 
the total operations for all resources in a resource group is set to > 1,000.
 1. Click the value in the Eligible operations > 1,000 with improvement components 
column to open the Search Algorithm Rule Component worksheet.
 2. In the Search Algorithm - Component Type column, change the value to something 
other than InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement.
Search Algorithm rule component is InsertionImprovement or 
SwapImprovement and the scoring mode is SchedulingEngine
The scoring mode for a Search Algorithm Rule is set to SchedulingEngine and the Search 
Algorithm rule component is set to InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement. This is an issue 
because these component types require larger numbers of iterations to succeed at improving 
the schedule.
 1. Click the value in the Improvement component and SchedulingEngine mode? column 
to open the Search Algorithm Rule Component worksheet.
 2. Do one of the following:
 l In the Search Algorithm - Component Type column, change the value to something 
other than InsertionImprovement or SwapImprovement.
 l In the Search Algorithm worksheet, for the Search Algorithm - Scoring 
Modecolumn, change the value to Approximate.
Search Algorithm rules with no components
A Search Algorithm rule must have at least one component associated with it.
 1. Click the value in the Search Algorithm rules with no components? column to open the 
Search Algorithm Rule worksheet.
 2. Click the Value column for that rule.
 3. Insert a new record in the Search Algorithm Rule Component worksheet for that rule.

===== PAGE 220 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Applicationextensions 210
Temperature cooling factor not between 0 and 1
The cooling factor controls the probability that the Scheduling algorithm will move to a 
candidate schedule with a worse score than the current schedule. Valid values are a non-zero 
number between 0 and 1. Large values allow the search algorithm to accept schedules with 
worse scores. The probability of accepting worse scores depends on the size of the difference 
between the schedule scores. The probability also decreases with each iteration unless this factor 
is set to 1.
 1. Click the value in the Temperature cooling factor not between 0 and 1? column to 
open the Search Algorithm Rule worksheet.
 2. Specify a value between 0 and 1 in the Search Algorithm - Temperature Cooling Factor 
column.
Secondary Resource
Secondary Resource Requirements with Default Quantity <= 0 but 
IsHeld is false
A secondary resource requirement group has no default quantity available but it is not set to be 
held. When a secondary resource is set to be held, route operation segments scheduled on that 
resource are held until it is available.
 1. Click in the Secondary Resource - Group column to open the Secondary Resource 
Requirements worksheet.
 2. Do one of the following:
 l Specify a value greater than 0 in the Default Quantity column for that secondary 
resource group.
 l Clear the IsHeld checkbox for that secondary resource group.
Shift Patterns/Interval
Shift intervals with day of pattern < 0 or > the total number of 
pattern days
Shift intervals must have a day of pattern defined as equal to or greater than 0. It also cannot be 
a value greater than the total number of days defined for the shift pattern. A day of pattern value 
of 0 represents the first day of the pattern. 
 1. Click in the DayOfPattern < 0, DayOfPattern > PatternDurationDays column to open 
the  Shift Intervals worksheet.
 2. Review and edit the Day of Pattern values accordingly.

===== PAGE 221 =====
211 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER3:EnterpriseSchedulingresources
Shift intervals with efficiency = 0
A shift interval efficiency value is a time-specific value that overrides the efficiency defined on 
the primary resources for this interval period. Shifter intervals must have an efficiency value 
greater than 0 defined.
 1. Click in the Is Efficiency = 0? column to open the  Shift Intervals worksheet.
 2. Edit the Efficiency value for the shift interval to be greater than 0.
Shift patterns without interval
Shift patterns must have a shift interval that defines the start and end times for each day of the 
pattern. 
 1. Click in the Shift Pattern Name column to open the  Shift Intervals worksheet.
 2. Insert a new record to define an interval for this shift pattern.

===== PAGE 222 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 212
CHAPTER 4: Logging
If logging is enabled in the system, Maestro tracks the performance of Enterprise Scheduling and 
displays results in the Enterprise Scheduling Analysis workbook. By default this logging is off and 
can be  turned on in the Configuration worksheet in the Enterprise Scheduling Workbench 
workbook.
In addition, Maestro monitors application container health, algorithm metrics and errors, and 
information about application extension versions deployed to the system. For more information 
about these logs, see the Maestro Administration Guide.

===== PAGE 223 =====
 l Enterprise Scheduling calculations
 l Enterprise Scheduling data model
Part 3: Enterprise Scheduling Data Model

===== PAGE 224 =====


===== PAGE 225 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 215
CHAPTER 5: Enterprise Scheduling calculations
Maestro generates production schedules using different methods that consider all of the 
parameters and constraints defined for the schedule, in addition to the approach of how you 
want to calculate the schedule (forwards or backwards). You can also enable the Search 
Algorithm option to apply an iterative solution to a schedule generated by forward scheduling.
Forward scheduling
The forward-pass dispatch rule heuristic generates a schedule by moving forward along the 
defined time horizon and scheduling operations on eligible resources using operation priorities 
determined by the assigned dispatch rule. Precedence relationships, resource capacity, size 
constraints, and other parameters are considered when scheduling each operation.
Search algorithm option
When enabled, the Search Algorithm option is applied to the preliminary scheduled that is 
generated either using the forward pass heuristic or using the random initial schedule mode. 
Depending on the parameters defined for the Search Algorithm option, operations are moved 
around in that preliminary schedule to create a new candidate schedule. The Search Algorithm 
option runs for a defined number of iterations and generates a candidate schedule for each 
iteration. Candidate schedules are measured on effectiveness using a calculated score, the 
objective function. Maestro searches for the schedule with the lowest objective function and 
selects is as the resulting schedule. See Search Algorithm option.

===== PAGE 226 =====
216 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
BOM Types
The Scheduling algorithm uses the BOM types defined in planning to specify the BOM 
relationships between component and assembly operations. Specifically, three rules for BOM 
types are interpreted by the algorithm: the effective date range when the BOM requirement 
should be fulfilled, what datetime value is used to apply the effectivity rule, and how required 
quantities are rounded to satisfy BOM requirements. See BOM Types.
Component consumption types
Users can assign a component consumption type to a part to specify what types of orders the 
part can consume to fulfill BOM requirements. They can also set component parts to be 
unconstrained. This lets the assembly parts that require that component ignore the available 
quantities of the component and be scheduled independently. See Component consumption 
types.
Backwards scheduling
The Scheduling algorithm starts by scheduling operations on downstream resources and then 
back scheduling its predecessor operations on upstream resources. Typically, backwards 
scheduling is applied to resources that are upstream from a bottle necked resource.
See Backward scheduling.

===== PAGE 227 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 217
Backward scheduling
When backward scheduling is enabled, the Scheduling algorithm takes two passes through the 
data: a forward pass and a backward pass.
Forward pass
The Scheduling algorithm takes an initial pass through the schedule data and uses constraints, 
defined properties, and specified parameters to generate a preliminary schedule. Properties for 
area groups, resources, and operations are considered along with parameters such as dispatch 
rules, shift patterns, and operation properties in determining how the preliminary schedule is 
organized.
Backward pass
The algorithm takes another pass through the schedule data for dependency constraints to align 
upstream operations, especially near bottlenecked operations.
Two fields control how backward scheduling functions:
 l ResourceGroup.BackwardSchedule: determines if backwards scheduling is used for that 
resource group.
 l AreaGroup.BackwardScheduleJustInTime: determines how backward scheduled are 
aligned to successor operations. 
 l When Y: predecessor operations are right-aligned and scheduled just-in-time to fulfill 
successor operations.
 l When N: predecessor operations are left aligned and scheduled as early as possible 
without being scheduled before a prior successor operation.
How operations are backward scheduled
Operations are scheduled in two steps:
 1. Successor operations to operations assigned to backward scheduled resource groups are 
scheduled while still accounting for parameters and constraints such as resource eligibility, 
shift patterns, downtimes. The successor is placed on the schedule at the earliest possible 
time while still respecting defined maximum or minimum wait durations between related 
operations.
 2. Then predecessor operations for backward scheduled resource groups are scheduled. 
Depending on what value was set for the AreaGroup.BackwardScheduleJustInTime field 
on the area group, the operation is right aligned to be just in time with its successor or it is 
scheduled at the earliest possible time on the schedule.

===== PAGE 228 =====
218 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
For example, operations 1A - D are initially scheduled by forward pass as follows with Operation 
1A scheduled first and then its successor operations scheduled after it.  However, Operation 1D 
must be completed by 8 AM. Currently it is late and finished production closer to 8:45 AM.
In the backward pass, Operation 1D is scheduled first and aligned to it's assigned completion 
time of 8 AM. It's predecessor operations are then scheduled accordingly.
Depending on how the parameters such as the BackwardScheduleJustInTime field, minimum 
wait durations for predecessors, and the Search Algorithm option are configured, backward 
scheduling schedules operations differently. See Backward scheduling interactions.
The DispatchRule.MaximumDueDatePullAhead value also impacts backward scheduling, See 
Backward scheduling and maximum due date pull ahead. 
Concurrent batches and backwards scheduling
You can fully utilize the capacity of resources by backward scheduling concurrent batches of 
operations. When a resource has concurrent batches scheduled on it and backwards scheduling 
is enabled, operations are scheduled as early as possible based on their predecessor end times. 
The MaximumConcurrentBatches value for the Resource must be set to >1.

===== PAGE 229 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 219
Note: If changeovers are scheduled between the batches, operations that are backward 
scheduled won’t run concurrently.

===== PAGE 230 =====
220 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Backward scheduling interactions
Depending on what other properties are configured on the schedule, backward scheduling 
behaves differently. The following tables describe scheduling behavior when backward 
scheduling, just-in-time, minimum wait durations, and the Search Algorithm option interact:
 l Just in time aligns predecessor operations with their successor operations. This property is 
set on at the Area Group level.
 l Minimum wait duration determines the minimum time allowed between predecessor and 
successor operations. This property is set on the Route Operation Predecessor.
 l Search Algorithm runs the Scheduling algorithm multiple times to find a more effective 
schedule.
When predecessor operations are in resource group A and successor operations are in resource 
group B:
Backward 
Schedule
Backward 
Schedule 
Just In Time
Search 
Algorithm 
option 
enabled?
Minimum 
wait 
duration 
defined?
How operations are scheduled
True False No No Predecessor operations in resource group 
A are scheduled at the earliest available 
time based on all constraints.
True True No No Predecessor operations in resource group 
A are scheduled at the latest possible time 
(right aligned to successor operations) 
based on all constraints
True False Yes No Predecessor operations in resource group 
A are scheduled at the earliest available 
time based on all constraints. Successor 
operations in resource group B are 
scheduled following the search algorithm 
output. 
True True No Yes Predecessor operations in resource group 
A are scheduled at the latest possible time 
(right aligned to successor operations) 
while still respecting the defined minimum 
wait duration.
True False No Yes Predecessor operations in resource group 
A are scheduled at the earliest possible 
time while still respecting the defined 
minimum wait duration.

===== PAGE 231 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 221
When operations are in resource group Z precede operations in resource group A and 
operations in resource group B succeed the operations in resource group B:
Backward 
Schedule
Backward 
Schedule 
Just In Time
How operations are scheduled
True True The precedence constraints between operations in resource groups Z and A are 
respected while respecting defined minimum and/or maximum wait times and 
have multiple predecessors.

===== PAGE 232 =====
222 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Backward scheduling and maximum due date pull ahead
The maximum due date pull ahead value for a resource determines how early an operation can 
be scheduled before its due date. This value is specified for the dispatch rule applied to the 
resource and represents the number of days before the due data that an operation can be 
considered for scheduling. 
When backward scheduling is enabled, the maximum due date pull ahead value is respected 
unless an operation is locked or has a start and/or end time specified. This ensures that 
precedence constraints are respected. 
In the example below, the schedule has a MaximumDueDatePullAhead  value of 0 which 
means that no limit is applied to when operations can be scheduled. Operations on Resource A 
are scheduled one after another and their successor operations on Resource B are also 
scheduled in succession.
When a MaximumDueDatePullAhead  value of 1 is set, then operations can't be scheduled 
more than 1 day before they are due. Scheduling starts with the successor operations on 
Resource B but takes into account the pull ahead constraint for predecessor operations on 
Resource A and produces the following schedule.
When Just in time mode is also enabled to schedule predecessors just in time they're needed by 
their successor operation, the schedule still respects the maximum due date pull ahead.

===== PAGE 233 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 223


===== PAGE 234 =====
224 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Part Source Effectivity
To make sure that operations are scheduled on resources associated with the primary part 
source and within the defined effectivity dates defined in supply planning, you can enable part 
source effectivity on route operations. This avoids the generation of unsafe or infeasible 
schedules. In situations where parts from different suppliers are only allowed on specific 
machines and are usable for fixed periods of time, enforcing part source effectivity makes sure 
that operations aren’t placed on the wrong resource at a time when that part is no longer valid. 
For an example of how data is configured to model using part source effectivity, see Example of 
configuring part source effectivity.
Resources are linked to part sources through constraints defined on part sources. In forward 
scheduling, to determine if an operation is feasible for scheduling, the Scheduling algorithm 
looks at the following:
Assigned PartSourceEffectivityType for the corresponding route operation
This determines how the Scheduling algorithm applies part source compatibility and effectivity 
windows when selecting a resource and starting time for the operation. 
For the part source, the operation must be scheduled on a resource that is linked to the part 
source referenced by the operation (ScheduledReceiptOperation.Scheduled
Receipt.PartSource or PlannedOrderOperation.PlannedOrder.PartSource).
For the operation’s start time, it must fall within the effective date range 
(PartSource.FirstEffectiveDate to PartSource.LastEffectiveDate) for any part source linked to 
the resource’s constraint.
Note: This part source might be different than the operation’s part source because this is 
linked to the resource.
Valid values for this field include:
 l Unconstrained: Neither part source compatibility or effectivity windows are enforced. 
Operations can be scheduled on any eligible resource at any time.
 l EffectivityWindow: Only the effectivity window is enforced.
 l EffectivityWindowAndOrderPartSource: Both part source compatibility and the 
effectivity window are enforced.
Assigned part source for the order
This is the part source assigned to the scheduled receipt or planned order 
(ScheduledReceiptOperation. ScheduledReceipt.PartSource or 
PlannedOrderOperation.PlannedOrder.PartSource) for the operation.

===== PAGE 235 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)PartSourceEffectivity 225
Resource constraint
This is the referenced constraint for the resource that the operation is assigned on.
Source constraints
These are the source constraints that reference the resource constraint and define the fixed and 
variable amounts of constraint required to produce supply from a specific part source.
Part source effectivity windows
This is the effectivity window for each valid part source linked to the order. An effectivity window 
is defined as the date range between PartSource.FirstEffectiveDate and the 
PartSource.LastEffectiveDate.
Note: Locked operations can override the above feasibility checks, however, any 
violations are logged in the Operation Feasibility Explanation and Trace Order 
Explanation worksheets in the Enterprise Scheduling Analysis workbook.

===== PAGE 236 =====
226 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Example of configuring part source effectivity
At a plant that manufactures frozen patties, three different suppliers are used to create three 
different patty products. Each supplier is represented as a part source:
 l Supplier V: provides vegan base material
 l Supplier M: provides mushroom base material
 l Supplier C: provides chicken base material
Production operations must be scheduled on separate resources to prevent cross-contamination 
between vegan and mushroom patties and chicken patties to avoid violating food-safety 
standards.
Each supplier has a defined constraint and an effectivity window that determines when the 
product is fresh and safe to use. The full date range that a part source is valid to be used is 
defined by its first and last effective dates.
This scenario would be modeled as follows:
Part sources
On the PartSource table, the following records define the part source and valid dates that the 
part from that source can be used. After the last effective date, the base material (vegan, 
mushroom, or chicken) will expire.
Source Part FirstEffectiveDateLastEffectiveDateResulting effectivity 
window
Supplier V Vegan base 
material
Jan 1 Feb 1 Jan 1 -> Feb 1
Supplier M Mushroom base 
material
Jan 1 Jan 3 Jan 1 -> Jan 3
Supplier C Chicken base 
material
Jan 1 Jan 7 Jan 1 -> Jan 7
Constraints
Each part source has the following defined constraints:

===== PAGE 237 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)PartSourceEffectivity 227
Name Description
Vegan food standard Parts that meet vegan-safe handling requirements.
Mushroom food standardParts that meet mushroom-handling and freshness requirements.
Chicken food standard Parts that meet chicken-handling safety requirements.
Source constraints
Each part source is mapped to one or more source constraints that define the fixed and variable 
amounts of constraint needed to produce supply from that part source.
Part Source 
(reference)
Constraint 
(reference)
Effective In 
Date
What this means
Supplier M Vegan food 
standard
Jan 1
Mushroom base material must meet both vegan and 
mushroom-safe handling requirements and these 
requirements take effect starting Jan 1.Supplier M Mushroom food 
standard
Jan 1
Supplier V Vegan food 
standard
Jan 1 Vegan base material must meet vegan-safe handling 
requirements and these requirements take effect starting 
Jan 1.
Supplier C Chicken food 
standard
Jan 1 Chicken base material must meet chicken-handling safety 
requirements and these requirements take effect starting 
Jan 1.
Resources
Each primary resource is mapped to a compatible constraint.
Name Constraint (reference)
Vegan1000 Vegan food standard
Mush1000 Mushroom food standard
Chick1000 Chicken food standard
Notes: 
 l If a part source is mapped to the same constraint as a primary resource, then 
operations that use that part source are eligible to be scheduled on that resource.

===== PAGE 238 =====
228 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
 l If a part source is mapped to multiple constraints and its part complies with all of the 
criteria of the constraints, then operations that use that part source can be scheduled 
on any primary resource that supports at least one of those constraints.
Route operations
These records specify how the scheduled receipt and planned order operations corresponding 
to defined route operations will behave in the schedule based on the value selected for the 
PartSourceEffectivityType field. Since we want to enforce both the part source and the 
effectivity windows, we will set the PartSourceEffectivityType value to 
EffectivityWindowAndOrderPartSource for all of our operations.
Name Part Source 
Source
Part Source Part Part Source Effectivity Type 
VeganRouteOpSupplier V Vegan base material EffectivityWindowAndOrderPartSource
MushRouteOp Supplier M Mushroom base 
material
EffectivityWindowAndOrderPartSource
ChickRouteOp Supplier C Chicken base material EffectivityWindowAndOrderPartSource
To ensure the following:
 l Vegan and mushroom patties are not cross-contaminated with chicken patties.
 l Mushroom patties can be processed by more than one compatible resource.
 l Operations are scheduled within effectivity windows so that patties don't expires. 
The above data configuration defines the following rules for the Scheduling algorithm:
 l Operations that use the part source Supplier M (mushroom) can be scheduled on either 
Vegan1000 or Mush1000 resources because their part complies with both the vegan and 
mushroom constraints. These operations must be scheduled within the effectivity window 
of Jan 1 to Jan 3.
 l Operations that use the part source Supplier V (vegan) can only be scheduled on the 
resource Vegan1000. These operations must be scheduled within the effectivity window of 
Jan 1 to Feb 1.
 l Operations that use the part source Supplier C (chicken) can only be scheduled on the 
resource Chick1000. These operations must be scheduled within the effectivity window of 
Jan 1 to Jan 7.

===== PAGE 239 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMTypes 229
BOM Types
Maestro uses Bill Of Material (BOM) types to define the types of bill records and the processing 
rules that determine their properties and behaviors. For example, the BOM type can determine 
how component part quantities are rounded or how an effective date range is applied to a 
BillOfMaterial record.
The Scheduling algorithm converts BillOfMaterial records into BOM requirement objects that 
are assigned work items. Each BOM requirement object specifies the quantity of component part 
required to produce the assembly work item.
The BOMType determines how the Scheduling algorithm interprets and fulfills BOM 
requirements for the following rules:
 l Effectivity rule: determines if the BOM requirement should be fulfilled by applying an 
effective date range.
 l Limit effective date: determines which datetime value, order due date or work item 
scheduling time, should be used when applying the effectivity rule.
 l Rounding rule: determines how the quantity required to satisfy the BOM requirement is 
rounded during BOM fulfillment.
Effectivity rule
Each BOM requirement has an effective date range that determines if it should be fulfilled that is 
determined by the effective dates specified for its BillOfMaterial record:
 l BillOfMaterial.EffectiveInDate: the start of the effective date range for the BOM 
requirement.
 l BillOfMaterial.EffectiveOutDate: the end of the effective date range for the BOM 
requirement.
The Effectivity rule determines how these dates are used when assessing the effectivity for a 
BOM requirement.
Depending on the value selected for this rule, the following setting is applied:
 l InclusiveExclusive: the due date of the assembly work item is compared against the 
date range. The requirement is fulfilled if (assembly.dueDate >= effectiveInDate AND 
assembly.dueDate < effectiveOutDate). This is the default setting.
 l Never: The effective date range is ignored, and the requirement is never fulfilled.
 l Always: The effective date range is ignored, and the requirement is always fulfilled.
 l Exclusive: The requirement is fulfilled if (assembly.dueDate > effectiveInDate AND 
assembly.dueDate < effectiveOutDate).

===== PAGE 240 =====
230 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
 l Inclusive: The requirement is fulfilled if (assembly.dueDate >= effectiveInDate AND 
assembly.dueDate <= effectiveOutDate).
The EffectivityRule is set to Exclusive which means that to fulfill this BOM requirement, the 
assembly due date must be less than the end of the date range. The due date is October 30 
which is = to the effective out date, but is not less than it. Accordingly the BOM requirement 
won't be fulfilled.
You can schedule operations only within the BOM effectivity date range by setting a constraint 
at the route operation level. See BOM effectivity constraints.

===== PAGE 241 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMTypes 231
Limit effective date
This rule determines which datetime value is used by the effectivity rule to check if a requirement 
should be fulfilled. Depending on the value specified for this field, one of the following datetime 
are used:
 l False: the due date and time for the assembly work item is used. This is the default setting.
 l True: the scheduling date and time is used. This datetime value is determined by when the 
BOM allocation to fulfill the BOM requirements is triggered:
 l If the work item hasn’t started, the event time is used as the scheduling datetime value.
 l If the work item has started, the work item start time is used as the scheduling datetime 
value.
This prevents operations within a work item using different scheduling dates and times.
In the example below, the effective date range is highlighted in yellow. Under Settings A, the 
LimitEffectiveDate is set to False. The BOM requirement won't be fulfilled because the assembly 
due date falls outside of the effective date range (On October 15th).
For Settings B, the LimitEffectiveDate is set to True which uses the scheduling datetime value to 
assess the BOM effectivity. The scheduling date and time (October 13th, 9AM) falls in the 
effective date range and therefore the BOM requirement will be fulfilled.


===== PAGE 242 =====
232 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Rounding Rule
To determine if a BOM requirement has been fulfilled, its required quantity is compared to the 
quantity allocated to fulfill it. The rounding rule determines how the required quantity is 
rounded before being compared to the allocated quantity.
Depending on the value chosen for this rule, the following setting is applied:
 l None: the required quantity is not rounded. This is the default setting.
 l Up: the required quantity is rounded up to the next integer.
 l Down: the required quantity is rounded down to the previous integer.
 l Nearest: the required quantity is rounded to the nearest integer. Values ending in 0.5 are 
rounded up.
 l QtyPerOrder: the required quantity is rounded to the quantity required to make a single 
assembly order.
The table below shows examples of how each of the rule settings are applied.
Rounding 
Rule Value
Required 
Quantity
Description Rounded 
Quantity
None 3.5 No rounding is applied. 3.5
Up 3.5 The required quantity is rounded up to the next integer. 4
Down 3.5 The required quantity is rounded down to the previous 
integer.
3
Nearest 3.5 The required quantity is rounded to the nearest integer. It 
ends in 0.5 so it is rounded up.
4
QtyPerOrder 3.5 The quantity per order is 2, so the required quantity is 
rounded down to 2.
2

===== PAGE 243 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints233
BOM effectivity constraints
You can control if operations are strictly scheduled within the BOM effectivity date range by 
configuring the ConstrainByBomEffectivity field at the route operation level and setting the 
pegging source to one of the following:
 l SupplyPlanning
 l SchedulingUpFront
 l SchedulingUpFront AND SupplyPlanning
When ConstrainByBomEffectivity = Y, the Scheduling algorithm only allows operations to be 
scheduled within the BOM's effective in and out dates.
When ConstrainByBomEffectivity = N, operations can be scheduled outside of the BOM's 
effective in and out dates.
Maestro checks for this BOM effectivity constraint and logs if operations passed or failed the 
check in the Operation Feasibility Explanation and Trace Order Explanation worksheets in the 
Enterprise Scheduling Analysis workbook.
Note: Locked operations remain locked even if they violate the BOM effective date 
range.

===== PAGE 244 =====
234 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Efficiency factors in scheduling
In Enterprise Scheduling, efficiencies can be defined at three levels:
 l Resource.Efficiency: a scaling factor applied to the primary resource.
 l ShiftInterval.Efficiency: value that overrides the efficiency applied to a resource that runs 
during that shift.
 l Downtime.Efficiency: value that overrides the efficiency applied to a shift interval when 
the downtime is applied in time period of that shift.
Shift interval efficiency
When primary resources run at different efficiencies at different times, you can apply an 
efficiency value to a shift interval to override the resource’s efficiency for the duration of that 
interval period. The efficiency is defined as a percentage in decimal format.
Downtime efficiency
You can also apply an efficiency to a specific time on a primary resource by applying an 
efficiency value on a downtime period. This value temporarily overrides the efficiency defined for 
the shift interval that the downtime is applied in.
Depending on the downtime efficiency value and shift interval overlaps when the intervals have 
efficiency values different than 1, the Scheduling algorithm applies the following behaviors.
Operation scheduled within the scheduling horizon, no shift interval 
overlap
Operation durations are multiplied by the downtime efficiency value. For example, in the 
diagram below Operation 1 is scheduled on Resource A from 5 - 6AM.


===== PAGE 245 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints235
A downtime is set for 4AM - 12PM on March 28 with an efficiency of 0.5. Since the operation is 
scheduled within that period, the downtime efficiency of 0.5 is applied changing it from a 1 hour 
to a 2 hour duration.
Operation scheduled partially within the scheduling horizon, no shift 
interval overlap
Operations within the scheduling horizon have their durations multiplied by the downtime 
efficiency value. However, operations outside of the scheduling horizon are unaffected.
For example, in the diagram below Operation 1 with a duration of 1 hour is scheduled on 
Resource A to run from 7:30 - 8:30AM.
A downtime is set for 4AM - 12PM on March 28 with an efficiency of 0.5. The part of the 
operation scheduled during that period (from 7:30 - 8AM) has the downtime efficiency applied 
to and now runs from 7 - 8AM and the part of the operation outside of that downtime period 
has the shift interval efficiency applied and continues to from 8 - 8:30AM. 

===== PAGE 246 =====
236 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Operation scheduled within the scheduling horizon, has shift interval 
overlap
Operation durations are multiplied by the downtime efficiency value.  The overlapping shift 
interval efficiency values are ignored. 
For example, in the diagram below Operation 1 is scheduled on Resource A from 5 - 6AM.
A downtime is set for 4AM - 12PM on March 28 with an efficiency of 0.5. Since the operation is 
scheduled within that period, the downtime efficiency of 0.5 is applied changing it from a 1 hour 
to a 2 hour duration.

===== PAGE 247 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints237
Operation scheduled partially within the scheduling horizon, has shift 
interval overlap
Operations within the scheduling horizon have their durations multiplied by the downtime 
efficiency value. The shift interval efficiency value is overridden. Operations that are outside the 
scheduling horizon are multiplied by the shift interval efficiency value.  
For example, in the diagram below Operation 1 with a duration of 2 hours is scheduled on 
Resource A to run from 6:30 - 8:30AM.
A downtime is set for 4AM - 12PM on March 28 with an efficiency of 0.5. The part of the 
operation scheduled during that period (from 6:30 - 8AM) has the downtime efficiency applied 
to and now runs from 5 - 8AM and the part of the operation outside of that downtime period 
has the shift interval efficiency applied and continues to from 8 - 8:30AM. 

===== PAGE 248 =====
238 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations


===== PAGE 249 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints239
Scheduling with component consumption 
types
Users can configure which component parts can be consumed to fulfill the BOM requirements 
for assembly parts by specifying which type of order (On Hand, Scheduled Receipt, or Planned 
Order) for a component part can be consumed. They can also choose to designate a component 
part is unconstrained to allow assembly parts that require that component to be scheduled 
independently of the availability of that unconstrained component.
For examples explaining how the Scheduling algorithm uses component consumption types to 
schedule operations, see the examples below.
Properties for the component consumption type are defined in the 
ComponentConsumptionType table and the type is then mapped to a specific part. Fields in the 
SchedulingWhereConsumed table track the consumption of component parts over the 
scheduling horizon. 
When a component becomes consumable to satisfy BOM requirements is determined by the 
value set for the component availability type field. It can be set to any of the following values 
when the pegging source for the schedule is set to SchedulingInProcess or 
SupplyPlanning:
 l ScheduledCompletion: components become consumable after their scheduled 
completion time.
Note: There are situations when assembly operations aren’t scheduled when a 
component order is finished, for example if the precedence rule applied to 
operation predecessor types is set to StartFromPredecessorStart, an assembly 
operation is scheduled when its component order operations start.
 l PlanningAvailableDate: components become consumable at the latest date on with their 
entire supply becomes available or at the latest time that they are scheduled.
 l LatestScheduledCompletionAvailableDate: components become consumable at their 
latest completion time and on the data when the entire supply is available.
 l Unconstrained: components are consumable at any time.

===== PAGE 250 =====
240 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Examples
The following examples describe how the Scheduling algorithm uses component consumption 
types when scheduling operations for the following BOM relationship. 
A single finished good (FG) is produced using two semi-finished goods (SFG1 and SFG2) that are 
required in a 1:1 ratio. SFG1 must be produced on station 1 and SFG2 on station 2. FG must be 
produced on station 3. 
The table below outlines the quantities of SFG1 and SFG2 available.
Example 1: Default component consumption type
When a component consumption type is not specified for either SFG, then the default 
component consumption type is used where all order types are consumable.
 l SFG1 has two consumable orders available: one scheduled receipt, one planned order
 l SFG2 has one consumable order available: one scheduled receipt
There are sufficient consumable quantities of the component parts to produce one FG.

===== PAGE 251 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints241
The following schedule is generated:
SFG1 and SFG2 are produced in parallel on different stations. Since FG has a BOM relationship 
with the SFGs, it must wait until both input SFGs are finished before it can start.
Example 2: User-specified component consumption type
SFG1 has been assigned a component consumption type that has the same settings as the 
default where all order types can be consumed. However, SFG2 has been assigned a component 
consumption type that only lets on hand orders be consumed. Scheduled Receipt and Planned 
Orders can't be consumed.
 l SFG1 has two consumable orders available: one scheduled receipt, one planned order
 l SFG2 has no consumable orders available. It only has one scheduled receipt quantity, but 
the component consumption type is set to only use on hand orders.
As a result, no FG can be produced.
 

===== PAGE 252 =====
242 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
The following schedule is generated:
SFG1 and SFG2 are still scheduled because scheduled receipt orders exist for both, but FG isn't 
scheduled because SFG2 can't be consumed according to the selected component consumption 
type.

===== PAGE 253 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints243
Example 3: Unconstrained consumption of SFG2
SFG1 has been assigned a component consumption type that has the same settings as the 
default where all order types can be consumed. SFG2 has been assigned a component 
consumption type that is set to only use on hand orders and has its component availability type 
set to unconstrained. 
 l SFG1 has two consumable orders available: one scheduled receipt, one planned order
 l SFG2 has no consumable orders available because it has 0 on hand orders, however 
because its component availability type is set to Unconstrained, this constraint is ignored.
A FG can be produced because SFG1 has a consumable order and SFG2 is unconstrained.
 
The following schedule is generated:

===== PAGE 254 =====
244 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
FG only depends on SFG1 now because SFG2 is set to unconstrained and is not longer required 
as a BOM input for FG. As a result, FG can be scheduled as soon as SFG1 finishes instead of 
waiting for both SFG1 and SFG2 to finish.
Example 4: Fully unconstrained consumption
When both SFG1 and SFG2 set to a component availability type of Unconstrained, the FG can be 
scheduled whether or not consumable quantities of SFG1 or SFG2 are available.

===== PAGE 255 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints245
The following schedule is generated:
FG can be scheduled concurrently with SFG1 and SFG2 since both of its inputs are unconstrained 
and it no longer has BOM dependencies that require it to wait for either SFG to finish.

===== PAGE 256 =====
246 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Component availability types and scheduling
In scheduling, the defined BOM requirements for a part source can be overridden when a 
component consumption type is assigned to a part. It determines which sources a component 
can consume from to fulfill demand, and you can specify which part source to use or to set 
component parts to be consumable at any time. When the pegging source for the scheduled is 
set to SchedulingInProcess or SupplyPlanning, component parts are scheduled differently 
depending on which component availability type is selected and whether the order has been 
scheduled. The table below outlines the different behaviors.
Scheduling Behaviour Component Consumption Types Component 
Order 
Scheduled?
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory at the component’s scheduled 
completion time.
ScheduledCompletion Yes
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory at the component’s scheduled 
completion time.
PlanningAvailableDate Yes
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory at the component’s scheduled 
completion time.
LastScheduledCompletionAvailableDate Yes
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory at the component’s scheduled 
completion time.  
Assembly orders can be scheduled even when no 
component orders are available in the inventory. 
Unconstrained Yes
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory on the component’s due date.
ScheduledCompletion No
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory on the component’s due date.
PlanningAvailableDate No
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory on the component’s due date.
LastScheduledCompletionAvailableDate No
Component orders are added to allocatable 
inventory on the component’s due date.
Unconstrained No
Note: The component’s scheduled completion time is when the order is completed on 
the schedule.

===== PAGE 257 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints247
Operation pausing and overlapping
On the schedule, operations are scheduled to run before or after downtimes or off-shift periods. 
You can configure Enterprise Scheduling to have operations pause and/or overlap over these 
non-working times. For example, if you want to perform a changeover on a machine during a 
downtime, you can schedule the operation segment to overlap so that its changeover will run 
during that downtime.
Maintenance actions can also be configured to pause and/or overlap with downtimes and off-
shift periods to allow you to run maintenance on a machine during those times.
Depending on the configuration, locked operation segments and maintenance actions behave 
differently in the scheduled. See Locked operation pausing and overlapping. 
Notes: 
 l For operation segments, resources must be configured to allow operation pausing 
and/or overlapping.
 l For maintenance actions, resources and the operation segment type assigned to the 
maintenance must be configured to allow operation pausing and/or overlapping.
 l Operation segments and maintenance actions can partially overlap over a downtime 
or off-shift period.
 l For operations  that start before  the scheduling horizon start date, their segments will 
overlap on resources irrespective of resource availability. This is because resource 
unavailability doesn't display on the Gantt chart before the horizon start.
 l For downtime periods that overlap on the schedule, the more constrained downtime 
is applied to the downtime period.
Operation pausing
When an operation segment or maintenance action is configured to pause over downtimes or 
off-shift periods, the operation starts before that time, pauses during it, and resumes after the 
downtime or off-shift is finished. Accordingly, the operation duration is extended over that 
period.
Operation overlapping
When an operation segment or maintenance action is configured to overlap over downtimes or 
off-shift periods, the operation can start before, during, or after the downtime or off-shift period.  
Resource availability is not constrained under this configuration to allow the segment or 
maintenance to run in parallel with that period.

===== PAGE 258 =====
248 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Note: Operations can't be manually dragged and dropped onto the Gantt chart to 
overlap over a downtime or shift period. This capability is automatically applied by the 
operation segment configurations.
Operation pausing and overlapping
When your schedule is configured to allow operation segments or maintenance actions to both 
pause and overlap over downtimes and off-shift periods, the operation will overlap over those 
times and can start before, during, or after the downtime or off-shift period. Resource availability 
is not constrained under this configuration to allow the segment or maintenance to run in 
parallel with that period.
Note: For operations that start before or on the scheduling horizon start date and 
complete after that date and have segments configure to pause over a downtime or off-
shift period, the pause will be ignored if it results in an end date for the segment that is 
later than its actual end date.
Pausing operation segments over maintenance actions
By default, operation segments are fully scheduled before or after a maintenance action. When 
pausing is enabled, the operation segment starts before the maintenance, pauses during it, and 
then resumes after it’s completed. Accordingly, the segment duration is extended over the 
maintenance action.
Operation segment and maintenance action interactions
Depending on how  operation segments and maintenance actions are configured, they are 
scheduled in different ways.
Operation segment and maintenance action pausing
When the following is configured for operation segments and maintenance actions:
Operation Maintenance action Downtime AND Off-shift Downtime OR Off-shift
Operation segmentPause Pause  
Maintenance action     Pause
The operation segment starts before any of those times,  pauses during them, and then resumes 
after the maintenance, downtime, or off-shift period is completed. Accordingly, the segment 
duration is extended over these periods.

===== PAGE 259 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints249
Operation segment pausing and maintenance action overlapping
When the following is configured for operation segments and maintenance actions:
Operation Maintenance action Downtime AND Off-shift Downtime OR Off-shift
Operation segmentPause   Pause
Maintenance action   Overlap  
The following applies:
 l The operation segment starts before those times, pauses during them, and then resumes 
after the maintenance and downtime or off-shift period is finished.
 l The maintenance action can start before, during, or after the downtime or off-shift period. 
Resource availability is not constrained under this configuration to allow the maintenance 
to run in parallel with the downtime or off-shift period.
Operation pausing and overlapping and maintenance overlapping
When the following is configured for operation segments and maintenance actions:
Operation Maintenance action Downtime AND Off-shift Downtime OR Off-shift
Operation segmentPause   Overlap
Maintenance action     Overlap
The following applies:
 l The operation segment and maintenance action start before those times, pauses during 
them, and resumes after the downtime or off-shift is finished.
 l The operation segment pauses over the maintenance action and resumes after the 
maintenance is completed. Accordingly, the segment duration is extended over the 
maintenance action.

===== PAGE 260 =====
250 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER5:EnterpriseSchedulingcalculations
Locked operation pausing and overlapping
Locked operation segments and maintenance actions display the same pausing and overlapping 
behavior as unlocked segments. See Operation pausing and overlapping. However, when a 
maintenance action is scheduled at the same time then the segments are scheduled differently.
Maintenance scheduled in middle of locked operation
When a maintenance is scheduled in the middle of a locked operation then the locked operation 
takes precedence and the maintenance action is scheduled at the first eligible time after the 
locked operation completes.
Operation segment pauses over locked maintenance
If an operation segment is configured to pause over a locked maintenance, then the segment 
starts before, pauses during, and resumes after the locked maintenance. Accordingly, the 
segment duration is extended over the locked maintenance action.
Maintenance pausing and locked operation segment pausing
When a maintenance action is configured to pause over a downtime or an off-shift period and a 
locked operation segment is configured to pause over downtimes, off-shift periods, and 
maintenance, then the locked segment starts before all of them, pauses during them, and the 
resumes after the periods and maintenance have completed. Accordingly, the segment duration 
is extended over those times.
Maintenance overlapping and locked operation segment pausing
When a maintenance action is configured to overlap over a downtime or an off-shift period and 
a locked operation segment is configured to pause over downtimes, off-shift periods, and 
maintenance, then the following applies:
 l The maintenance action can start before, during or after those periods. Resource availability 
is not constrained under this configuration to allow the maintenance to run in parallel with 
that period.
 l The locked operation segment starts before all of them, pauses during them, and the 
resumes after the periods and maintenance have completed. Accordingly, the segment 
duration is extended over those times.
Maintenance overlapping and locked operation segment pausing and 
overlapping
When both a maintenance action and a locked operation segment are configured to overlap 
over downtimes or off-shift periods and the locked segment pauses over the maintenance 
action, then the following applies:

===== PAGE 261 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)BOMeffectivityconstraints251
 l The maintenance action and locked operation segment can start before, during or after 
those periods. Resource availability is not constrained under this configuration to allow the 
maintenance and operation segment to run in parallel with that period.
 l The locked operation segment pauses over the maintenance and resumes it has completed. 
Accordingly, the segment duration is extended over the maintenance action.
Locked operations and locked start times
When a locked operation has a working segment with a locked start time, then the operation 
segment is scheduled at the locked time if its feasible. Otherwise, the segment is scheduled 
immediately after the locked time while still respecting all  constraints.
Note: To lock an operation that overlaps with downtimes or off-shift periods, you must 
manually set its locked Start Date Time.

===== PAGE 262 =====


===== PAGE 263 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG) 253
CHAPTER 6: Enterprise Scheduling data model
Enterprise Scheduling is populated by data that is typically a combination of data extracted from 
ERP/MES and manual intro into Maestro. The application relies on input and calculated tables in 
the Scheduling namespace, and has dependencies on the following tables in the 
MFG namespace:
 l Core::ControlSet
 l Mfg::BillOfMaterial
 l Mfg::BomAlternate
 l Mfg::Part
 l Mfg::PartSource
 l Mfg::PlannedOrder
 l Mfg::ScheduledReceipt
 l Mfg::Site
The Scheduling namespace includes all of the input, calculated, and control tables required for 
the Enterprise Scheduling application. They represent the master data, transactional data, and 
constraints for the application.
 l Control tables
 l Input tables
 l Calculated tables

===== PAGE 264 =====
254 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER6:EnterpriseSchedulingdatamodel
Note: To reduce the risk of accidental or incorrect data being added during data imports 
or modifications, automatic creation of related (reference) records has been limited in all 
Enterprise Scheduling tables except for the Property table. The Property table in the 
Enterprise Scheduling namespace still allows automatic reference record creating to 
allow for the efficient creation of new properties.

===== PAGE 265 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Controltables 255
Control tables
Control tables contain rules and values that determine the calculations performed on input data 
by Maestro which is then used to populate the data in calculated tables. Control tables are 
configured during a Maestro deployment and ensure that algorithm calculations match those 
found in your enterprise or host systems. Only system or data administrators can modify these 
values after their initial configuration. For simulation purposes, worksheets based on Control 
tables can be modified within a scenario.
Each control table contains a Value field that defines the value used in the corresponding Type 
field of the base table. For example, the Type fields in the Changeover, RouteOperationSegment, 
and ScheduledReceiptOperationSegment tables reference the Value field in the 
OperationSegmentType table.
The Enterprise Scheduling data model includes the following control tables:
BOMType table
The BOMType table is a control table that specifies the different types of bill records and the 
processing rules for them. The BOMType.Value is a text string assigned to each parent-child 
record in the BillOfMaterial table. Each BOMType.Value defined represents a set of 
configuration rules that control the behavior for that parent-child relationship.
Note: For a complete list of BOMType table settings that are either unsupported or do 
not function as expected in Supply chain optimization. See Supply chain optimization 
BOMType control table limitations.

===== PAGE 266 =====
256 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER6:EnterpriseSchedulingdatamodel
Field name Description Data type Key
BlowThrough Specifies whether to use phantom BOM 
relationships or "Blow Through" bill logic. 
Dependent demand generated through this 
Bill record bypasses the netting of the 
component. Valid values are:
 l Y
 l N
Default value: N
Notes: 
 l This setting is ignored (always treated as 
N) on BOMType records used to define 
co-product or by-product configurations.
 l This field is supported by Supply chain 
optimization except in BOM substitutions 
through phantom parts, also known as 
blowthrough parts. See Supply chain 
optimization BOMType control table 
limitations.
Boolean  
ControlSet The control set associated with this 
BOMType record.
Referenced table: ControlSet
Reference Yes

DateRule When determining bill date effectivity, this 
field specifies which date to test for. Valid 
values are:
 l AnchorDate: The supply record's anchor 
date (ScheduledReceipt.AnchorDate) is 
used to determine which components of 
the BillOfMaterial are effective for 
explosion purposes. 
 l BlowThroughCalcDueDate:  
BillOfMaterial effectivity is determined 
using either the 
ScheduledReceipt.ReschedDate or 
PlannedOrder.DueDate of the real 
assembly. Unless the assembly is a 
phantom, this option is identical to 
CalcDueDate.
 l CalcDueDate: The FirstEffectiveDate 
and LastEffectiveDate values on the 
BillOfMaterial record are compared 
using the 
ScheduledReceipt.ReschedDate or 
PlannedOrder.DueDate to determine 
which BillOfMaterial records will have 
dependent demand generated on them.
 l CalcDockDate: A supply order's 
dependent demands are based on the 
components that are effective at the 
supply order's calculated dock date. That 
is, the FirstEffectiveDate and 
LastEffectiveDate values on the 
BillOfMaterial records are compared 
using either 
ScheduledReceipt.ReschedDockDate or 
PlannedOrder.DockDate.
 l CalcStartDate: The supply record's 
calculated start date 
(ScheduledReceipt.CalcStartDate) is 
used to determine which components of 
the BillOfMaterial are effective for

explosion purposes.
 l Ignore: The supply record ignores the 
BillOfMaterial.EffectiveInDate and 
BillOfMaterial.EffectiveOutDate 
resulting in the component always being 
considered for explosion.
Default value: Ignore
DependentDemand
Model
Specifies the model to use when the parent 
model should not be used in order BOMs.
When set to null, the model of the dependent 
demand is identical to the model of the 
parent. When this field is set to a model 
name, the referenced model is the model of 
the dependent demand.

EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Controltables 259
Field name Description Data type Key
DependentUnit
Rule
Specifies how dependent demands are 
handled with unit effectivity. If your company 
has not enabled Model-Unit Effectivity, this 
field is ignored. Valid values are:
 l Split: Split dependent demand 
according to the unit number range. 
Demands are not split if the supply's unit 
is less than zero.
 l First: Calculate the entire dependent 
demand according to StartUnit on the 
supply record. Do not split the order 
according to unit effectivity.
Default value: Split
Notes: 
 l This setting is ignored on BOMType 
records that define co-product or by-
product configuration.
 l This field is not supported by Supply 
chain optimization. See Supply chain 
optimization BOMType control table 
limitations.
String
(Enum)
 
Description A text description of the BOMType.Value.
Default value: blank string
String  
Table 6.1: BOMType (Mfg) fields

===== PAGE 270 =====
260 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER6:EnterpriseSchedulingdatamodel
Field name Description Data type Key
EffectivityRule Specifies for each BOMType how ranges of 
units and dates are interpreted when 
determining effectivity. This field applies to all 
effectivity rules (ranges apply to dates and 
unit ranges). Valid values are:
 l InclusiveExclusive: the record is 
effective starting on start and ending 
before the end (recommended method).
 l Never: The record is ignored regardless of 
all dates and Model and Unit Effectivity 
rules.
 l Always: Ignore all other effectivity rules 
and treat Bill as active.
 l Exclusive: Use effectivity dates and 
units if applicable (not effective on the 
dates or units).
 l Inclusive: Effective from start to end 
inclusive.
Default value: InclusiveExclusive
String 
(Enum)
 
Table 6.1: BOMType (Mfg) fields

===== PAGE 271 =====
EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)Controltables 261
Field name Description Data type Key
ExpiryRelationship Defines the relationship between this 
assembly and component combination 
during material expiration. This setting allows 
a given component's expiry to be treated 
differently depending on assembly and 
enables overriding certain expiry logic that is 
otherwise dictated by ExpiryRule settings 
when calculating expiry-related dates 
(particularly as it pertains to dependent 
expiry types). 
For example, if an assembly uses an 
ExpiryRule of Dependent, a specific 
component under that assembly can be 
subject to expiry without its expiration having 
any impact on the ExpiryDate of the 
assembly's supply. Similarly, dependent 
demands generated on component supplies 
under a Dependent assembly can ignore the 
EarliestExpiryDate on the assembly's driving 
supply. This setting also 
Valid values are:
 l None: No expiry relationship is assumed. 
Regardless of their ExpiryRule settings, 
the assembly and component defined on 
this record do not affect each others 
expiry dates. That is, the earliest expiry 
date on supplies of the component is 
always based on it's own shelf life 
requirements (not the assembly's) and 
expiry dates on this component's supplies 
never impact expiry of the assembly (only 
expiry dates on supplies from 
components with a Normal setting will 
impact the supply expiry date on 
dependent assembly types). This setting 
allows component and assembly expiry to 
be treated independently of one another.
 l Normal: A normal expiry relationship is 
assumed. Based on the relevant 
String
(Enum)
 
Table 6.1: BOMType (Mfg) fields

===== PAGE 272 =====
262 EnterpriseSchedulingApplicationConfigurationGuide(ADG)CHAPTER6:EnterpriseSchedulingdatamodel
Field name Description Data type Key
ExpiryRule settings, the assembly and 
component defined on this record can 
affect each others expiry dates. That is, 
earliest expiry dates can be passed down 
to dependent demands on the 
component and expiry dates on 
component supplies can be used in 
calculating expiry on the assembly. This 
setting corresponds to how assembly 
component relationships were always 
treated in expiry logic prior to Service 
Update 2404. 
See Specify expiry relationship between 
assembly and component (limited 
availability).

NEW  This field supports a recent change in 
expiry logic (limited availability)

as of Service Update H2503.
ExplodeNegatives Specifies how negative quantities per each of 
the BOMTypes are handled. Valid values are:
 l Y: Negative QuantityPer is used in the 
explosion calculation.
 l N: Negative QuantityPer is not used in 
the explosion calculation.
Default value: N
Note: This setting is ignored on BOMType 
records that define a co-product or by-

since QuantityPer values are required to be 
negative on all BillOfMaterial records that 
define co-products or by-products).
